Facts.text: Alaric II, King of the Visigoths, promulgates the Lex Romania Visigothorum (or Breviary of Alaric), a collection of Roman law
Pope John XII crowns German King Otto I the Great Emperor
Guido di Borgogna elected Pope Callistus II
Battle of Lincoln: English King Stephen captured by forces loyal to Empress Matilda and commanded by Robert, 1st Earl of Gloucester
Battle of Mortimer's Cross: in a major battle of the War of the Roses Yorkist army of Edward, Earl of March, defeats Lancaster force led by Jasper Tudor
Pedro de Mendoza founds Argentine city of Buenos Aires
Portuguese under Christovão da Gama capture a Muslim-occupied hillfort in northern Ethiopia in the Battle of Baçente
Edward Seymour, Duke of Somerset and former Lord Protector is freed from the Tower of London
M Rossi's opera "Erminia sul Giordano" premieres in Rome
Zorilla's "El más Impropio Verdugo Para Las" premieres in Madrid
New Amsterdam becomes a city, later renamed New York City
Dutch tragedy "Lucifer" by Joost van den Vondel, and regarded as his masterpiece, premieres in Amsterdam - banned three days later
Nicholas Rowe's play "The Tragedy of Jane Shore" premieres in London
King Frederik Willem I moves Lutherans towards East-Prussia
Federal St Theater, Boston, becomes 1st in US destroyed by fire
1st leopard exhibited in US, Boston (admission 25 cents)
Russian settlers establish Ft Ross trading post, north of San Francisco
Madman Jonathan Martin sets York Cathedral on fire, does £60,000 damage
US & British settlers in Oregon Country choose government committee
1st Chinese immigrants arrive by boat in San Francisco, California
Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ends the Mexican–American War. Mexico loses 55 percent of its territory to the US, including California, Nevada and Utah in exchange for $15 million.
1st British public men's toilet opens in Fleet St, London
Alexandre Dumas Jr's play, an adaptation of his novel "La Dame aux Camélias" premieres in Paris
Pope Pius IX encyclical "On persecution of Armenians"
The "Organized Incorporated Territory of Nevada" is created, lasting until October 31st, 1864
-Oct 7th) Cruise of CSS Florida
American industrialist James Oliver invents removable tempered steel plow blade
Cardiff Giant (supposed petrified human) proved to be gypsum
Baseball's National League forms at the Grand Central Hotel, NYC with teams in Boston, Chicago, Cincinnati, Hartford, Louisville, New York, Philadelphia, and St Louis
Greece declares war on Turkey
SS Strathleven arrives in London with first shipment of frozen Australian mutton
First Groundhog Day observed at Gobbler's Knob, Punxsutawney, Pennsylvania
Frank Sprague opens the first successful U.S. electric street railway system, the Richmond Union Passenger Railway, in Richmond, Virginia
Bottle cap for beverages patented by US inventor William Painter - still used today
Johnny Briggs takes a hat-trick, England v Australia at the SCG
Longest boxing match under modern rules: 77 rounds in Nameoki, Illinois, between Harry Sharpe and Frank Crosby
US warship Kearsarge wrecked on Roncador Reef, near Solomon Island
The Australian Premiers' Conference held in Melbourne decides to locate Australia's capital (Canberra) between Sydney and Melbourne.
Gustave Charpentiers opera "Louise" premieres at the Salle Favart, in Paris France; performed by the Opéra-Comique conducted by André Messageri
Female Army Nurse Corps established as a permanent organization
Mexican government troops are ambushed by Yaqui Indians, 100 killed
The US Congress passes the Army Reorganization Act, placing the minimum number of men under arms at 58,000
Pope encyclical against separation of church & state
Italian writer Filippo Tommaso Marinetti publishes "Manifest o of Futurism" in Paris, France
Frederick R. Law, parachutes from Statue of Liberty (stunt for Pathe)
NYC's Grand Central Terminal train station opens
James Royce Shannon's musical "Shameen Dhu" premieres in NYC
Monarchist riot in Portugal
France occupies (German) Memel territory
Tarto/Dorpat peace treaty: USSR recognizes Estonian independence
Ethyl gasoline 1st marketed, Dayton, Ohio
US signs friendship treaty with Central American countries
International Ski Federation (FIS) forms
Belgian episcopacy rejects liberalism, communism & socialism
Dogsleds reach Nome with emergency diphtheria serum after 1000-km
NL holds Golden Jubilee Year meeting at same hotel where NL began
3 men dance Charleston for 22 hours
Harry Tierney and Joseph McCarthy's musical "Rio Rita" premieres in NYC
Ziegfeld Theater (Loew's Ziegfeld) opens at 6th Ave & 54th St NYC
1st siyyum of Talmud celebrated by Daf Yomi students
1st use of a rocket to deliver mail (Austria)
Geneva disarmament conference begins with 60 countries

Reconstruction Finance Corporation (RFC) organized in the US to provide financial support to state and local governments and to make loans to banks, railroads, mortgage associations and other businesses
Ucicky's "Rotten Morning" premieres in Berlin
Dutch Roman Catholic Bishops warn against fascism and Nazism
Leonarde Keeler first uses his polygraph machine on criminals, who are later convicted of assault based on its findings in Portage, Wisconsin
LA Times urges security measures against Japanese-Americans
US auto factories switch from commercial to war production
German 6th Army surrenders after the Battle of Stalingrad in a major turning point in Europe during World War II
MLB Chicago Cubs return to original uniform after experimenting with a vest style
4th US marine division conquers Roi, Marshall Islands
Allied troops 1st set foot on Japanese territory
Baseball meets in NYC to discuss postwar action
Edward Chodorov's play "Decision" premieres in NYC
Escape attempt at Mauthausen concentration camp
The Proclamation of Hungarian Republic made
President Harry Truman urges congress to adopt a civil rights program
1st broadcast of "What's My Line" on CBS-TV
-35°F (-37°C), Greensburg, Indiana (state record until 1994)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Rio Grande College basketball forward Bevo Francis scores NCAA record 113 points vs Hillsdale College
Snow falls on Gibraltar
1st presidential news conference on network TV-Eisenhoweron ABC
The Coasters sign with Atlantic Records
UN adopts a resolution calling for Israeli troops to leave Egypt
Syria joins Egypt in United Arab Republic
WRIK (now WLUZ) TV channel 7 in Ponce, PR (PTC) begins broadcasting
Michale Eufemia sinks 625 balls in pool match without a miss
Dutch Prince Bernhard opens new RAI building in Amsterdam
1st pole vault over 16' (4.88m) (John Uelses-16', Melrose Games)
8 of 9 planets align for 1st time in 400 years
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
British pop singer Helen Shapiro begins an English tour, the Beatles are part of undercard
GI Joe, debuts as a popular American boy's toy
Sjoukje Dijkstra (Netherlands) wins Olympic gold for figure skating
Joe Orton's play "Loot" premieres in Brighton
Pakistan suggests six-point agenda, with Kashmir dispute as number one item, for proposed Indo-Pak ministerial talks after 1965 war
Bolivia adopts its constitution
Formation of American Basketball Association is announced
Springer Publishers in West Berlin, bombed
KMST TV channel 46 in Monterey-Salinas, CA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Stan Coveleski & Waite Hoyt are voted into Baseball Hall of Fame
US Congressional Black Caucus organizes with Charles C. Diggs Jr. its first Chair
Angry demonstrators burn the British Embassy in Dublin to the ground in protest at the shooting dead of 13 people on 'bloody sunday'
Richard Helms, ends term as 8th director of CIA; succeeded by James R Schlesinger (until July)
Playmobil toys debut at the Nuremberg Toy Fair, designed by Hans Beck and produced by the Brandstätter Group
Smallest crowd at Cleveland Arena (Cavs vs Golden State-1,641)
The F-16 Fighting Falcon flies for the first time.
Army offensive against rebels in Eritrea
"Rich Little Show" debuts on NBC-TV
Roger Connor, Fred Lindstrom and umpire Cal Hubbard elected to Baseball Hall of Fame
Soviet Salyut 4 Space Station re-enters Earth's atmosphere
Toronto Maple Leafs Ian Turnbull scores 5 goals, NHL record for a defenseman
FBI releases details of Abscam, a sting operation that targeted 31 elected & public officials for bribes for political favors
Mexican boxer Salvador Sánchez dethrones Danny “Little Red” Lopez as WBC world featherweight champion with a 13th-round TKO in Phoenix, AZ; Lopez champion since November 1976, 8th defense
Government troops and Muslim fundamentalists battle in Hamah, Syria
CBS' premiere of fact based WW2 drama "The Scarlet and the Black", based on the life of Monsignor Hugh O'Flaherty
Lebanese army fights in Beirut
Dalai Lama meets PopeJohn Paul IIin India
Oscar Arias Sanchez elected president of Costa Rica
KC Royal pitcher Dennis Leonard (3X 20 game winner), retires
Philippines adopts constitution
0°F (-18°C) or below in 15 US states
South African PresidentF. W. de Klerkpromises to free Nelson Mandela and legalizes ANC and 60 other political organizations
Aravinda De Silva scores 267 v NZ at Wellington
NH snaps its 32-game losing streak at home beating Holy Cross, 72-56
US postage is raised from 25 cents to 29 cents
Australian Kieren Perkins swims 1,500m freestyle short course world record, 14:32.40 at AIS, Canberra, Australia
Danny Everett runs world record 400m indoor (45.02 sec)
Henry Olonga no-balled for throwing in Zimbabwe-Pakistan Test Cricket
"Men Are From Mars, Women Are From Venus" closes at Gershwin NYC
Daniel Baldwin hospitalized in NYC for cocaine overdose
Philippine DC-9 crashes apparently killing all 104 on board
Russian pop girl duo Tatu start a four-week run at #1 on the UK singles chart with "All The Things She Said"
Cold snap across Europe kills more than 100 people (over 400 people by 08-02)
MV Rabaul Queen sinks off the coast of Papua New Guinea with 246 people saved and 126 missing (100 of these estimated to be trapped inside)
NHL player Sam Gagner becomes the first player to scores eight points in one game for the Edmonton Oilers against the Chicago Blackhawks since 1989
18 people are killed and 34 are injured after a bus catches fire after falling down a ravine in Gansu province, China
23 people are killed and 8 are injured after militants attacked an army base in the Lakki Marwat District, Pakistan
Protests in Ukraine turn violent after parliament passes legislation that outlaws protest
First case of Zika contracted on US mainland (Texas) and second known sexually transmitted case confirmed in Texas
All 955 miners rescued from the Beatrix gold mine in Welkom town, South Africa, after 2 days underground
More than 40 mummies from 323-30 BC found at a burial site at Tuna el-Gebel archaeological site south of Cairo, Egypt
Virginia Governor Ralph Northam admits to wearing blackface in 1984 but says he's not in a photo of men wearing blackface and a Ku Klux Klan robe on a yearbook page
Palindrome Day: the date 02022020 reads the same forward and backward including in the US and China (last one like this 11 November 1111)
Alejandro Mayorkas is the first Latino and immigrant to be confirmed as head of the US Department of Homeland Security
Pete Buttigieg is the first openly gay person to be confirmed to a US cabinet post as transportation secretary
Russia's Sputnik V COVID-19 vaccine deemed 91.6% effective according to analysis published in "The Lancet"
Russian opposition leader Alexey Navalny jailed for three and a half years in jail over alleged parole violations in Moscow
More than one million Afghans have fled the country for Iran since October due to the country's economic crisis, according to immigration authorities threatening a new migrant crisis
Wildfires begin burning in Chile, east of Viña del Ma, spread to Quilpué and Villa Alemana, killing at least 131 with hundreds missing. Chile's greatest natural disaster since 2010.
First recorded performance ofWilliam Shakespeare's comedy play "Twelfth Night", to celebrate Candelmas in the Middle Temple Hall, London (diary of John Manningham)
George Frideric Handel's opera "Poro" premieres at the King's Theatre in London, England
British government ofRobert Walpoleresigns
Thomas Arne's opera "Artaxerxes" premieres  at the Theatre Royal, Covent Garden in London, England
Joseph Haydn's 102nd Symphony in B premieres at the King's Theatre in London, England
Gioachino Rossini's opera "Semiramide" first performed at La Fenice in Venice, Lombardo-Venetian Kingdom
American poetJoyce Kilmerwrites his famous poem "Trees" in Mahwah, New Jersey
James Joyce's novel "Ulysses" is first published by Sylvia Beach in Paris (1,000 copies)
Australian spin bowlerClarrie Grimmetttakes 14 wickets v South Africa (7-116 & 7-83)
Hermann Goeringbans Communist meetings and demonstrations in Germany
Frank Sinatraperforms at the Lyric Theater in Indianapolis, Indiana, his first as featured singer with theTommy DorseyOrchestra
American golfing champBen Hoganseriously injured in an auto accident
B.B. King's “3 O’Clock Blues” hits #1 on the US Billboard's R&B hit parade to become his first national hit
Hubert de Givenchypresents his first collection in Paris with Bettina Graziani opening the show
"The Nutcracker" ballet choreographed byGeorge BalanchinewithMaria Tallchiefas the Sugar Plum Fairy opens in New York, establishes its popularity in the US
PresidentEisenhowerannounces the detonation of the world's 1st hydrogen bomb (tested in 1952)
Despite a severe ankle injuryTenley Albrightleads an American 1-2 in the women's figure skating at the Cortina d'Ampezzo Winter Olympics; Carol Heiss takes the silver medal
Buddy Holly's last performance at Surf Ballroom in Clear Lake, Iowa
Vince Lombardisigns a 5 year contract to coach NFL Green Bay Packers
Elected to Baseball Hall of Fame - Red Faber, Burleigh Grimes,Tim Keefe, Heinie Manush, John Montgomery Ward, & Miller Huggins
Louisiana State University'sPete Maravichbecomes 1st to score 3,000 college basketball points
GeneralIdi Aminousts Milton Obote and appoints himself President and Dictator of Uganda
Lefty Gomez, Ross Youngs & William Harridge selected for Baseball Hall of Fame
Tom Stoppard's play "Jumpers" premieres in London
"The Midnight Special" late night rock music show debuts on NBC-TV; performers include Ike andTina Turner,Curtis Mayfield, Don Mclean, the Byrds, and George Carlin
Test debut of champion New Zealand cricket all-rounder SirRichard Hadlee; drawn 1st Test v Pakistan, Wellington
Barbra Streisand's first #1 hit, "The Way We Were"
PopePaul VIencyclical "To Honor Mary"
"The HoneymoonersSecond Honeymoon" airs on TV
Chicago Archbishop Joseph Bernardin is among 18 new cardinals named by PopeJohn Paul II
F. W. de KlerkreplacesP. W. Bothaas South Africa's National Party leader
Stingscores his second UK #1 album with 'The Soul Cages'
IRS &Willie Nelsonsettle on $9M tax bill (of $16.7M)
Frito Lay pays court ordered $2,500,000 to singer-songwriterTom Waitsfor using his distinctive voice song style in radio advertisement
Václav Havelbecomes the first president of an independent Czech Republic, after the split with Slovakia
Scotty Bowmanbecomes first coach in NHL history to win 1,000 games
Philip Glass' Symphony No. 6 (Plutonian Ode), for soprano and orchestra, based on the poem byAllen Ginsberg, premieres with
Jennifer Lopezstarts a three week run at #1 on the US singles chart with "All I Have"
"RuPaul's Drag Race" hosted by RuPaul premieres on Logo TV
Shinzō Abe, Japan’s Prime Minister vows to defend the Senkaku Islands "at all costs"
Super Bowl XLVIII, MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford, NJ: Seattle Seahawks beat Denver Broncos, 42-8; MVP: Malcolm Smith, Seattle, LB
American singerR. Kellyarrested after turning himself in on ten counts of aggravated criminal sexual abuse, including with three minors
Super Bowl LIV, Hard Rock Stadium, Miami Gardens, FL: Kansas City Chiefs beat San Francisco 49ers, 31-20; MVP:Patrick Mahomes, KC Chiefs, QB; Chiefs' 1st victory in 50 years
Jeff Bezosannounces he is stepping down as CEO of Amazon after 30 years, becoming executive chairman
US PresidentJoe Bidensigns executive orders to reunite immigrant families, setting up a new taskforce to address around 1000 remaining separated families
67th Grammy Awards:Beyoncéwins Album of the Year and Best Country Album for "Cowboy Carter",Kendrick Lamarwins Song and Record of the Year for "Not Like Us",Chappell RoanBest New Artist
By this date at least 200 people a day were being buried in London as a result of the Black Death
Arthur St. Clair is elected the 9th President of the Continental Congress under the Articles of Confederation
Queen Victoria's funeral takes place in St. George's Chapel, Windsor Castle, England
Norway’s Thorleif Haug takes the cross-country double at the Chamonix Winter Olympics when he wins the 18k event; Haug also 50k gold medalist

James I of Aragon, also known as 'James the Conqueror', King of Aragon (1213-76), KIng of Majorca (1231-76), and King of Valencia (1238-76), longest Iberian ruler at 62 years, born in Montpellier, France (d. 1276)
John, King of Denmark (1481-1513) and Norway (1483-1513), born in Aalborghus Castle, Aalborg (d. 1513)
Bona Sforza, Queen of Poland and Grand Duchess of Lithuania (1518-48), born in Vigevano, Milan (d. 1557)
Damião de Góis, Portuguese philosopher, humanist, and historian, born in Alenquer, Portugal (d. 1574)[1][2]
René de Birague, Italian-French lieutenant-general, chancellor and cardinal, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1583)[1]
Lodovico Ferrari, Italian mathematician known for solving the quartic equation, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1565)[1]
Anna Roemers Visscher, Dutch poet, born in Amsterdam (d. 1651)
Jacob van Campen, Dutch painter and architect (New Church, Amsterdam), born in Haarlem (d. 1657)
Gabriel Naudé, French librarian and scholar, personal librarian to Cardinal Mazarine, assembled his Bibliothèque Mazarine, born in Paris (d. 1653)
Noël Chabanel, French Jesuit, missionary to the Hurons and one of the eight Canadian Martyrs, born in Saugues, Kingdom of France (d. 1649)[1]
Johannes Schefferus, Alsatian-born Swedish humanist and archaeologist, born in Strasbourg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1679)
Benedict XIII [Pietro Francesco Orsini], Italian 245th Pope (1724-30), born in Gravina, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1730)
Nell [Eleanor] Gwyn, English actress and mistress to King Charles II, born in London, England (d. 1687)
William Phips, North American treasure hunter, military man and administrator (1st Governor of Massachusetts Bay Colony), born in Nequasset, Massachusetts Bay Colony (d. 1695)
Louis Marchand, French Baroque organist and composer, born in Lyon, France (d. 1732)
William Borlase, British naturalist and antiquary (The Antiquities of Cornwall, The Natural History of Cornwall), born in Cornwall, England (d. 1772)[1]
François de Chevert, French general (War of Austrian Succession), born in Verdun, France (d. 1769)
Wenzel Anton, Prince of Kaunitz-Rietberg, Austrian-Czech diplomat and statesman in the Habsburg monarchy, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1794)
Gottfried August Homilius, German organist and composer (Weihnachtsoratorium), born in Rosenthal, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1785)
Hannah More, English religious writer, poet, and philanthropist (Cheap Repository Tracts), born in Bristol, England (d. 1833)
Christian Gottfried Thomas, German composer, publisher, and musicologist, born in Wehrsdorf,  Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1806)
Vincenc Tucek, Bohemian composer, conductor and singer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1821)
Johannes van den Bosch, Dutch colonial administrator (Governor-General of Dutch East Indies 1830-33), born in Herwijnen, Dutch Republic (d. 1844)
Jacques Philippe Marie Binet, French mathematician, physicist and astronomer, born in Rennes, France (d. 1856)[1][2]
Jean-Baptiste Boussingault, French agricultural chemist (the nitrogen cycle), born in Paris, France (d. 1887)
Albert Sidney Johnston, American Confederate General, born in Washington, Kentucky (d. 1862)
Leopold Eugen Mechura, Czech composer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1870)
Nathaniel Collins McLean, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1905)
John Glover, English chemist (sulfuric acid), born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1902)
Jose Maria de la Purificacion Ventura, Spanish composer (Father of the modern Sardana), born in Alcalá la Real, Spain (d. 1875)
Hans E Schack, Danish politician and writer (Phantasterne - Fantasy), born in Sengeløse, Denmark (d. 1820)
Abner Monroe Perrin, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army, born in Edgefield District, South Carolina (d. 1864)
Oswald Aschenbach, German landscape painter, born in Düsseldorf, Prussia (d. 1905)
Alfred Brehm, German zoologist, writer, and first director of the Zoological Garden of Hamburg (Brehms Tierleben / Brehm's Animal Life), born in Renthendorf, German Confederation (d. 1884)[1][2]
Luigi Manzotti, Italian mime artist, dancer, and ballet choreographer, born in Milan, Lombardo-Venetian Kingdom (d. 1905)
Louis Bourgault-Ducoudray, French Breton composer, born in Nantes, Frances (d. 1910)
François-Alphonse Forel, Swiss physician, scientist, and founder of limnology, the study of lakes, born in Morges, Switzerland (d. 1912)[1]
Yulian-Karl Vasilievich Sokhotsky, Russian mathematician known for his work on complex analysis and special functions, born in Warsaw, Russian Empire (d. 1927)[1]
Leander Schlegel, Dutch composer and pianist, born in Oegstgeest, Netherlands (d. 1913)
Jules François Blasini, Curaçaon-Corsican pianist and composer, born in Dutch Colony of Curaçao (d. 1887)
Pavol Országh Hviezdoslav, Slovak poet, playwright, and translator (Hájnikova Žena / The Gamekeeper’s Wife), born in Vyšný Kubín, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1921)[1][2][3]
Carel Victor Gerritsen, Dutch politician and free thinker (husband of suffragette Aletta Jacobs), born in Amersfoort, Netherlands (d. 1905)
José Guadalupe Posada, Mexican artist and illustrator (Biblioteca del niño mexicano), born in  Aguascalientes, Mexico (d. 1913)
Teunis Stoel, Dutch actor and director (Small Theater), born in Rotterdam (d. 1914)
Makar Ekmalyan, Armenian composer (Patarag - Divine Liturgy of Armenia), born in Vagharshapat, Erivan Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1905)
Jan Six, Dutch art historian and art collector (Six Collection), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1926)
Havelock Ellis, English physician and social reformer who studied sexual behaviour (Psychology of Sex), born in Croydon, England (d. 1939)
Curtis Guild, Jr., American politician, journalist and soldier, 43rd Governor of Massachusetts (1906-09), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1915)
Solomon R. Guggenheim, American businessman and philanthropist (Guggenheim Museum NYC), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1949)
Johan Paul van Limburg Stirum, Dutch colonial administrator and diplomat (Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies 1916–1921), born in Zwelle, Netherlands (d. 1948)
Konstantin von Neurath, German politician and convicted Nazi war criminal (Foreign Minister 1932-38), born in Kleinglattbach, German Empire (d. 1956)
Leo Fall, Austrian operetta composer (Dollar Princess), born in Olmütz, Austrian-Hungarian Empire (d. 1925)
Annette Kolb, German author (Small Fanfare) and pacifist, born in Munich (d. 1967)
Friedrich-Max "Fritz" Kreisler, Austrian-American violinist and composer (Alt-Wiener Tanzweisen), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1962)
Christian Gauss, American professor and writer (Phi Beta Kappa award namesake), born in Ann Arbor, Michigan (d. 1951)
Karl Frederick, American pistol shooter (Olympic gold 1920), born in Chateaugay, New York (d. 1963)
Andrew, Prince of Greece and Denmark, born in Monte Carlo, Monaco (d. 1944)
Mikhail Gnesin, Russian composer (Vrubel; The Youth Of Abraham), born in Rostov-on-Don, Russia (d. 1957)
S Z Sakall [Gerö Jenö], Hungarian stage and film character actor (Dolly Sisters, Casablanca), born in Budapest Hungary (d. 1955)
Julius Deutsch, Austria politician and general in the Spanish republican army, born in Lackenbach, Austria-Hungary (d. 1968)
Aldo Palazzeschi [Giurlani], Italian writer (I cavalli bianchi), born in Florence, Italy (d. 1974)
Kurt Baschwitz, German-Dutch sociologist, journalist and pioneer of mass communication research (Du und die Masse / You and the Mass), born in Offenburg, German Empire (d. 1968)[1]

Ernst Hanfstängl, German-American businessman, art dealer, and early supporter of Adolf Hitler who later provided Allies with valuable information about the dictator, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1975)
Candelario Huízar, Mexican violist, horn player, and classical and canción composer ("Surco"), born in Jerez, Zacatecas, Mexico (d. 1970)
Frederick Lane, Australian swimmer (Olympic gold 200m freestyle, 200m obstacle 1900), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1969)
Jean de Lattre de Tassigny, French general (WWII, Indo-China), born in Mouilleron-en-Pareds, France (d. 1952)
Frank Foyston, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame center (Stanley Cup 1914 Toronto Blueshirts, 1917 Seattle Metropolitans, 1925 Victoria Cougars), born in Minesing, Ontario (d. 1966)
Cornelius Lanczos, Hungarian mathematician, born in Székesfehérvár, Hungary (d. 1974)
Len Doyle, American actor (Harrington-Mr District Attorney), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1959)
Kazimierz Kuratowski, Polish mathematician known for his work on topological and metric structures, born in Warsaw, Russian Empire (d. 1980)[1]
Howard Johnson, American hotelier (Howard John's chain), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1972)
Josephina Johanna "Fien" de la Mar, Dutch actress (Theatre De la Mar), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1965)
Benny Rubin, American actor and comedian (Benny Rubin Show), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1986)
Asa Smith Bushnell III, American sports executive (Secretary US Olympic Committee 1945-65), born in Springfield, Ohio (d. 1975)
Carroll Righter, American columnist and astrologer to the stars, born in Salem, New Jersey (d. 1988)
Connie Gilchrist, American actress (Cry Havoc, Letter to 3 Wives), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1985)
Jascha Heifetz, Russian-American violin virtuoso (Carnegie Hall debut -1917), born in Vilnius, Russian Empire (d. 1987)
Newbold Morris, American politician, lawyer, and first president of the New York City Council (1938-45), born in New York City (d. 1966)[1]
Tyra Ryman, Swedish actress (Luffar-Petter, Peter the Tramp), born in Arboga, Sweden (d. 1990)
Frank McGrath, American actor (The Reluctant Astronaut, Wagon Train), born in Mound City, Missouri (d. 1967)
Buzurg Alavi, Iranian writer (Panjah va sih nafar, Namal'ha), born in Tehran, Persia (d. 1997)
Jose Enrique Pedreira, Puerto Rican composer ("Tus Caricias"; "Siempre"), and pedagogue, born in San Juan, Puerto Rico (d. 1959)

Ayn Rand [Alisa Zinov'yevna Rosenbaum], Russian-American author, screenwriter and philosopher who developed the philosophical system of "Objectivism" (Atlas Shrugged, The Fountainhead), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1982)
Jean-Pierre Guerlain, French business strategist, associate director (1933-70) and chief executive (1970-81) of Guerlain House, founded by the Guerlain family, born in Paris, France (d. 1996)[1]
Bernardas Brazdzionis, Lithuanian poet, editor and critic, born in Stebeikėliai, Lithuania, Russian Empire (d. 2002)
Cal Tinney, American comedian and actor (The Missouri Traveler, Stop Me If You Heard This One), born in Pontotoc County, Oklahoma (d. 1993)
Pavel Sivic, Slovenian composer, born in Radovljica, Slovenia (d. 1995)
Renzo Rossellini, Italian composer, born in Rome, Italy (d. 1982)
Frank Albertson, American actor (Psycho; It's a Wonderful Life, Alice Adams), born in Fergus Falls, Minnesota (d. 1964)
Charlotte Auerbach, German-born British geneticist and zoologist, born in Krefeld, Germany (d. 1994)
Jean-Jacques Grunenwald, French organist and composer, born in Cran-Gevrier, France (d. 1982)
Jussi Bjorling, Swedish operatic tenor, born in Borlänge, Sweden (d. 1960)
Burton Lane, American film and stage composer and lyricist (Finian's Rainbow), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Millvina Dean, British civil servant who was the last living survivor of the RMS Titanic and also the youngest aboard, born in Branscombe, Devon, England (d. 2009)
Stefan Schnabel, German actor (Anna, Dracula's Widow, Firefox), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1999)
Poul Reichhardt, Danish actor (Soldaten og Jenny, Det gælder os alle), born in Ganløse, Denmark (d. 1985)
Eric Kierans, Canadian economist, liberal politician, and MP (1963-68), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2004)[1]
Abba Eban [Aubrey Solomon Meir Eban], Israeli diplomat, ambassador to US (1950-59), and scholar (Diplomacy for the Next Century), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 2002)[1][2]
Evert Werkman, Dutch journalist (Het Parool), born in Hellendoorn, Netherlands (d. 1988)[1]
Stan Leonard, Canadian golfer (3 PGA Tour titles), born in Vancouver, BC (d. 2005)
Xuân Diệu, Vietnamese poet (Thơ thơ / Young Love), born in Tuy Phuoc, French Indochina (d. 1985)[1][2]
Hella Haasse, 'Grande Dame of Dutch Literature' (Oeroeg, Cider for Poor People), born in Batavia, Dutch East Indies (d. 2011)
Lisa Della Casa, Swiss operatic soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1953-67), born in Burgdorf, Switzerland (d. 2012)
Bonita Granville, American actress and producer (These Three, Hitler's Children, Lone Ranger), born in New York City (d. 1988)
